coredata.dc:title: Quantitative detection of captopril in urine by smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Lixia
Liu, Ting
Cui, Di
Yi, Jiaqi
Jiang, Wei
Li, Xiaoshuang
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: Captopril (CP) is a widely used antihypertensive drug. In this study, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform on the basis of ratiometric fluorescent test strips was developed, which can accomplish visualization for the quantitative detection of captopril. Ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed from carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs). After adding Cu2+ to fluorescent probe, Cu2+ can complex the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of Au NCs and aggregate Au NCs, which will quench the fluorescence of Au NCs. Compared with amino and carboxyl groups, -SH in CP has a higher affinity for Cu2+ and can capture Cu2+ to restore Au NCs fluorescence. In this process, CDs remained essentially unchanged as background fluorescence. As CP concentration increased, the fluorescence color showed a distinct change from blue to purple to orange. Based on this principle, a sensing platform combining smartphone and fluorescent test strips was constructed to visualize the quantitative detection of CP by RGB values. Under optimal conditions, the wide linear range of CP detection for both fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone paper-based sensing platform was 0.25–50 μM. The limits of detection were as low as 76 nM and 101.3 nM, respectively. Furthermore, it was implemented successfully for the detection of CP in urine. The satisfactory recoveries were 96.0–103.3% and 92.0–108.0% for fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone platform, respectively. This smartphone-assisted platform provided a new approach for visual detection of CP, which showed its great potential in bioanalytical assays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Captopril
Fluorescent test strip
Smartphone platform
Visual detection
Urine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing CP based on smartphone platform
Detecting of CP in urine samples
Characterization of the CDs and Au NCs
Determination of CP by ratiometric fluorescent probe
Detection of CP in urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of fluorescent test paper for visual detection of captopril.
(a) TEM image of Au NCs, (b) Cu2+ ions added in Au NCs, (c) CP added in the solution of (b), (d) TEM image of CDs, the size distribution histogram of (e) Au NCs and (f) CDs.
XRD of (a) CDs, (b) GSH-Au NCs, FT-IR of (c) GSH and GSH-Au NCs, (d) CDs.

The fluorescent spectrum of the CDs/Au NCs with different concentrations of Cu2+. The fluorescence intensity ratios of Au NCs to CDs were adjusted to (a)1.5:1, (b)1:1 and (c)1:2, Figure (d) was a photograph of the corresponding fluorescent solution of (a), (b) and (c) taken under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra (λ ex = 310 nm) of the ratiometric fluorescent probe with Cu2+ (c(Cu2+) = 2.5 μM)) after adding different concentrations of CP, the insets were taken under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, (b) Linear plot of fluorescence intensity ratio (I615/I458 = 1:1) and different concentrations of CP.
(a) A series of changes in the color of fluorescent test paper after adding different concentrations of CP. (b) The linear graph of the value of R/B and different concentrations of CP.
Detection of CP in human urine by fluorescent test strips.
Comparison with other reported methods for the detection of captopril.

head.ce:title: Quantitative detection of captopril in urine by smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization
Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Captopril (CP) is a widely used antihypertensive drug. In this study, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform on the basis of ratiometric fluorescent test strips was developed, which can accomplish visualization for the quantitative detection of captopril. Ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed from carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs). After adding Cu2+ to fluorescent probe, Cu2+ can complex the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of Au NCs and aggregate Au NCs, which will quench the fluorescence of Au NCs. Compared with amino and carboxyl groups, -SH in CP has a higher affinity for Cu2+ and can capture Cu2+ to restore Au NCs fluorescence. In this process, CDs remained essentially unchanged as background fluorescence. As CP concentration increased, the fluorescence color showed a distinct change from blue to purple to orange. Based on this principle, a sensing platform combining smartphone and fluorescent test strips was constructed to visualize the quantitative detection of CP by RGB values. Under optimal conditions, the wide linear range of CP detection for both fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone paper-based sensing platform was 0.25–50 μM. The limits of detection were as low as 76 nM and 101.3 nM, respectively. Furthermore, it was implemented successfully for the detection of CP in urine. The satisfactory recoveries were 96.0–103.3% and 92.0–108.0% for fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone platform, respectively. This smartphone-assisted platform provided a new approach for visual detection of CP, which showed its great potential in bioanalytical assays.

ce:section.ce:para: Captopril (CP) is an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor (ACE inhibitor or ACEI). CP has a lowering effect on systolic and diastolic blood pressure, which is a commonly used antihypertensive drug for tertiary hypertension. However, when too much CP is used, it can lead to kidney damage, circulatory failure and other symptoms [1]. Hence, it is imperative to develop a precise and sensitive strategy for the determination of captopril to guide the clinical use of the drug. Up to date, the main methods for the determination of CP are capillary electrophoresis [2], atomic absorption [3], high performance liquid chromatography [4], chemiluminescence [5] and fluorescence method [6]. In spite of the fact that these methods are highly sensitive, the detection must be performed in a laboratory with costly instruments, which is not suitable for analysis of CP in real samples on site. Therefore, it is essential to set up a rapid analysis method for detecting CP.

Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging fluorescent nanomaterials with outstanding optical characteristics, high quantum yield, photostability, biocompatible properties and enhanced chemical inertness [7]. Our group has a good foundation in the application of CDs detection. Fu synthesized biomass CDs from animal bones to form multichannel arrays capable of recognizing metal ions [8]. Yin detected cytochrome c and trypsin in serum with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots synthesized by enzymatic hydrolysis of lignin [9]. Most of the previously reported CDs-based fluorescence detection are single-emission sensors, which are easily interfered by various factors such as photoelectric system drift, sample concentration and autofluorescence. However, single-color fluorescence changes limit detection accuracy and visual discrimination. In contrast, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with dual emission not only overcomes these factors, but also can achieve semi-quantitative measurements through changes in fluorescence color. CDs can also combine with some fluorescent materials to form a dual-emission fluorescent sensor, thereby reducing the background interference. Gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) are nanoclusters composed of several or dozens of gold atoms, which have the advantages of high stability, good biocompatibility and have received wide attention [10]. Although previous studies have Au NCs with dyes constituting ratiometric fluorescent probes [11–13]. Regrettably, dyes tend to have poor water solubility and fluorescence stability and suffer from photobleaching. Compared with dyes, CDs is regarded as ideal reference signal. On this basis, CDs and Au NCs were compounded to make a ratiometric fluorescent sensor in our experiments.

The emergence of smartphones has greatly facilitated people’s lives, which also provides new ideas for researchers and creates more possibilities. In the meantime, paper-based sensors are inexpensive, productive and environmentally friendly, making them candidates for easy and fast detection. In recent years, researchers have integrated smartphones with fluorescent test strips to detect different target analytes [14]. For instance, Zhao et al. [15] constructed a smartphone-portable platform based on glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters for the analysis of thiram residues. Maruthupandi et al. [16] reported a copper nanocluster used as a fluorescent probe, which combined with a smartphone-assisted paper kit to detect Hg2+ and S2- in water samples. Recently, in our group Liu et al. [17] established a smartphone sensing platform on the basis of fluorescein isothiocyanate and cadmium telluride quantum dot ratiometric fluorescence sensors, realizing the rapid visual detection of Ag+ in water samples. These research works promote the development of smartphone-based detection technology. Especially in developing countries and remote areas, low-cost smartphone-assisted paper-based sensing platforms are essential to enable on-site detection [18].

Herein, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 , we constructed a new captopril sensing strategy based on a combination of a smartphone applet and ratiometric fluorescent test strips. In our work, the ratiometric fluorescent probe was composed of CDs and Au NCs. The orange fluorescence of Au NCs can be quenched by Cu2+ due to coordination effect between Cu2+ and Au NCs surface functional groups. On the contrary, CDs as internal reference signals showed good fluorescence stability. Then the addition of CP resulted in the quick recovery of Au NCs orange fluorescence because of the stronger affinity with Cu2+ to -SH of the CP. With the implementation of the above ratiometric fluorescent probe detection strategy on the test paper, distinct fluorescent color changes could be observed. The color changes of these fluorescent test papers could be captured and converted to RGB values in time by the smartphone. Smartphone-assisted portable sensing platform provided a powerful tool for visual and quantitative detection of CP in the urine sample analysis. It has the significance of guiding the appropriate dosage of medication for patients in different body.

Captopril, reduced glutathione (GSH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), glycine (Gly), L-leucine (Leu), phenylalanine (Phe), L-threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), L-valine (Val) and isoleucine (Ile) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Boric acid (H3BO3), acetic acid (HAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), CuCl2, K2SO4, Ca(NO3)2, CH3COONa were obtained from Kermel (Tianjin, China). And The Britton-Robison (BR) buffer solution was prepared by mixing the appropriate quantity of H3PO4, H3BO3, HAc and NaOH. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), citric acid, ethylenediamine were obtained from RHAWN (Shanghai, China). Urea and glucose (Glu) was ordered from Guangfu (Tianjin, China). The chemicals were prepared as received without extra purification. The ultrapure water was produced form a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA).

The X-ray diffraction spectra were collected on the Shimadzu XRD-600 diffractometer (Kyoto, Japan). The size and shape of nanomaterials were used to obtain by a H7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The characterization of the structures was performed on FT-IR360 (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). The UV absorption spectra (from 200 to 800 nm) were obtained by TU-1901 spectrometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). The fluorescence spectra (from 400 to 800 nm) were recorded by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan).

The blue-emission CDs were obtained via a previous method with some modification through a one-step strategy [19]. In brief, 510 μL of anhydrous ethylenediamine and 1.575 g of citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL water. After mixing well, the solution was transferred to the hydrothermal reactor and reacted for 5 h at 200 °C. The obtained liquid in reactor was cooled down to ambient temperature and filtered by the 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Finally, the solid powder of CDs was obtained by rotary evaporator, which was then dissolved in water for subsequent use.

The orange-emission Au NCs were obtained on the basis of earlier report with some modification [20]. The glassware utilized in the experiment process was first immersed in aqua regia and then ultrasonic cleaning with ethanol and ultrapure water. Specifically, the fresh GSH (10 mM, 3 mL), HAuCl4 (10 mM, 2 mL) and ultrapure (5 mL) water were mixed. After the solution became colorless within 5 min, it was gently stirred and heated for 24 h at 70 °C. After centrifugation at 6000 rpm/min for 5 min, the supernatant was collected. Subsequently, the Au NCs were dialyzed (1000 Da) overnight. Finally, the solution of Au NCs were lyophilized to powder. The obtained solid was dissolved in water for further use.

For the detection of CP, a fluorescence “on–off-on” method was introduced. The ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed from CDs, Au NCs with the fixed fluorescence intensity ratio. First, 45 μL of CDs solution, 55 μL of Au NCs solution, 500 μL of BR buffer solution (pH = 7, 10 mM) and 2.5 μM of Cu2+ solution (100 μL) was mixed to the quartz cuvette reacting for 2 min. Subsequently, 500 μL different concentrations of CP (0.25–50 μM) solution were added to the above solution, mixed well and reacted for 2 min. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra were measured from 400 to 800 nm at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. Corresponding images were recorded by a smartphone (HONOR 30S) camera under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp in the dark box. Each sample was measured three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing CP based on smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para: The cut circular test strips (diameter: 2.0 cm) were immersed in total of 2 mL ratiometric fluorescent probes solution and Cu2+ solution for 2 h. After soaking, the test strips were taken out and dried at room temperature in the dark. Finally, different concentrations of CP solution (0.25–50 μM) were added dropwise to the dried test papers and the fluorescence color of the test papers were observed under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. In order to minimize experimental error, the photos were captured by the smartphone (HONOR 30S) camera in the dark box. Moreover, the distance between the UV lamp and the fluorescent test strips was fixed, and the smartphone was placed about 10 cm away from the test strips. The fluorescent test strips were identified RGB values through a smartphone WeChat applet (color recognition extractor).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting of CP in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of the established strategy, we used a standard addition method to detect captopril in urine. The urine was obtained from healthy volunteers and impurities were removed using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The resulting urine was added to a range of different concentrations of captopril. The procedure for detecting CP in actual samples was the same as in Sections 2.4, 2.5. The three independent experiments were performed at each spiked concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs and Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to characterize the morphologies and particle sizes of fluorescent materials. As depicted in the Fig. 2 (a), 2(b) and 2(c), we investigated the morphology of Au NCs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ or CP by TEM. Similar to Au NCs, CDs also showed monodispersed and spherical in Fig. 2(d). Corresponding illustrations of Au NCs and CDs showed distinct lattice stripes. The lattice spacings were 0.224 nm and 0.218 nm, respectively. The particle size distribution diagrams were shown in Fig. 2(e) and 2(f). The average particle sizes for CDs and Au NCs were 3.25 nm and 1.74 nm, correspondingly.

Moreover, in Fig. 3 (a), the X-ray diffraction (XRD) of Au NCs had a broad peak at 2θ = 21.0°, indicating the resulting Au NCs were small and had poor crystallinity [21]. From Fig. 3(b), the XRD broad peak of CDs was at 2θ = 21.4°, which was due to the amorphous carbon structure and graphite lattice spacing [22].

FT-IR was used to analyze the respective functional groups of CDs and Au NCs. According to the FI-IR spectra in the Fig. 3(c), the peak of GSH at 2523 cm−1 represents the S-H stretching vibration. Compared to GSH, the disappearance of the SH characteristic peak of GSH-Au NCs indicates that the SH bonds of GSH-Au NCs were broken. Meanwhile, the stable Au-S bonds were formed, which enabled GSH to be immobilized on the Au NCs surface [23]. As depicted in Fig. 3(d), the functional groups of CDs were studied as well. The peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 3242.9 cm−1 were belonged to stretching vibrations of the OH, NH, respectively. The characteristic absorption peak of CO stretching vibration was centered at 1663.9 cm−1. The peak at 1553 cm−1 was due to the CN stretching vibration or NH bending vibration, which indicated the presence of secondary amide groups. Moreover, the peaks at 1481.1 cm−1 and 1395.8 cm−1 put down to the characteristic absorption band of CC, CH stretching vibration, respectively [24].

The elements of the synthesized fluorescent materials were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). As depicted in Fig. S1(a), characteristic peaks at 86.08 eV, 154.08 eV, 287.08 eV, 401.08 eV and 534.08 eV were belonged to Au 4f, S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, respectively. The spectrum (Fig. S1(b)) of Au 4f region was able to be separated into two peaks, belonging to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, separately. The peaks correspond to Au (0) (83.68 eV, 87.28 eV) core and Au (1) (84.18 eV, 87.78 eV) shell [25]. Fig. S1(c) showed the full XPS spectrum of CDs. The strong characteristic peaks were at 285.27 eV, 400.62 eV, and 531.73 eV. They represent C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. From the results of peak splitting of C1S in Fig. S1(d), it was clear that the carbon elements on the surface of CDs mainly forms sp2 hybridized CC/CC (284.26 eV), CN (285.64 eV) and CO (287.15 eV). The remainder of the XPS data for CDs was shown in Fig. S1(e) and S1(f). XPS spectra illustrated the successful nitrogen doping of CDs.

The optical characteristics of the synthesized CDs and Au NCs were investigated by Fluorescence Spectrometer and UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. The two obvious absorption peaks were observed near 240 nm and 340 nm for CDs as shown in Fig. S2(a), which may belong to π-π* leap of CC/CN bonds and n-π* leap of CO bonds [26]. Likewise, in Fig. S2(b), Au NCs presented a weak peak at around 400 nm [27]. The Fig. S2(c.) and S2(d) showed the excitation spectra of the two fluorescent materials, respectively. The excitation peaks of CDs were at 213, 247, 351 nm with the emission of 458 nm, while the excitation peaks of Au NCs were at 280 nm with the emission of 615 nm. When the excitation wavelength was changed from 280 nm to 390 nm (Fig. S2(e)), the fluorescence intensity of CDs showed an increasing followed by decreasing trend, achieving the maximum at 340 nm. Different from CDs, the Au NCs fluorescence intensity performed a tendency of decreasing followed by increasing and then decreasing, achieving the optimum at 270 nm in Fig. S2(f). In order to guarantee the simultaneous excitation of both fluorescent materials, the optimal excitation wavelength for the ratiometric fluorescent probe was chosen as 310 nm.

The response of fluorescent probe with different fluorescence intensity ratios to Cu2+ (0–2.5 μM) was investigated. With the addition of Cu2+, the Au NCs fluorescence was gradually quenched, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained stable as a background reference. When the Cu2+ concentration reached 2.5 μM, the fluorescence quenching degree of Au NCs reached the maximum.

To enable a wider color variation, the incorporation ratio of blue and orange fluorescent materials needs to be controlled. A series of fluorescence intensity ratios of Au NCs to CDs (I615/I458 = 1.5:1, 1:1, 1:2) were explored in Fig. 4 . As the fluorescence intensity ratio was 1:1, the fluorescence solution color transformed from orange to purple to blue. Compared to I615/I458 = 1:1, the other two fluorescence intensity ratios exhibit relatively narrow color variations. It turned out that 1:1 was more suitable as the optimal fluorescence intensity ratio for subsequent detection.

As shown in Fig. S3(a), the pH of the before and after addition of Cu2+ to the fluorescent probe, probe with Cu2+ and CP was investigated. The range of pH = 3–10 (3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10) was chosen, so as to research the impact of pH change on the testing system. With the addition of Cu2+, it was obvious that fluorescence quenching efficiency best was at pH = 7. Moreover, the best fluorescence recovery efficiency was also achieved at pH = 7. When the pH value was over 7, it was possible that Cu(OH)2 precipitation was produced. In a comprehensive comparison, pH = 7 was chosen as the best acidity.

The photostability verification experiment of individual CDs, Au NCs and ratiometric fluorescent probe was also investigated. Fluorescence spectra were recorded every five minutes under a 365 nm UV lamp. Slight fluorescence changes were observed within 60 min in Fig. S3(b), S3(c) and S3(d), which indicated that CDs and Au NCs had good resistance to photobleaching and stability. Dynamic experiments showed in Fig. S3(e) that the fluorescence response was very fast after the addition of Cu2+. However, after fluorescence quenching within 2 min, the fluorescence remained essentially unchanged. The Au NCs fluorescence was recovered within 2 min after the addition of captopril, as shown in Fig. S3(f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CP by ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions of detection, the different concentrations of CP were added to the ratiometric fluorescent probe with Cu2+. As clearly observed in Fig. 5 (a), the fluorescent intensity of Au NCs in the system gradually restored. Additionally, the fluorescent colors gradually changed from blue to orange when concentration of CP increased (Fig. 5(a) inset). The values of I615/I458 were closely related to the CP concentration and excellent linearity was shown in the range of CP concentration from 0.25 to 50 μM in the Fig. 5(b). The regression equation is: I615/I458 = 0.014c + 0.13 (R2 = 0.992). The detection limit (LOD) is 76 nM, which was calculated by LOD = 3σ/k, where σ was the standard deviation of the signal of 10 blank parallel measurements and k was the slope of calibration curve.

The produced fluorescent test paper showed uniform blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp in Fig. 6 (a). Different concentrations of CP were added to the paper-based sensor. Because of the observation with the naked eye, the uncertainty was relatively large. To improve accuracy, a smartphone applet (color recognition extractor) was chosen to capture the RGB values of the fluorescent test strips. We evaluated the relationship between R/B values and CP concentration as shown in Fig. 6(b). Moreover, the quantitative relationship can be formulated by the following equation: R/B = 0.023c + 0.24 (R2 = 0.994). LOD was 101.3 nM, which was calculated by LOD = 3σ/k. The paper-based sensor had the advantages of portability and simplicity of operation.

To evaluate the sensing mechanism for detecting CP, it was investigated in two parts. Fluorescence spectra, TEM and UV absorption spectra were carried out. First, as demonstrated in Fig. 4(b), the orange fluorescence of Au NCs was quenched with the increased of Cu2+ concentration. The quenching of Au NCs fluorescence by Cu2+ may be due to the charge transfer mechanism [28]. With paramagnetic effect, Cu2+ can easily coordinate with amino or carboxyl groups [29,30]. There were massive of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of GSH, which had strong coordination interactions with Cu2+ and can complex Cu2+ in the Au NCs surface [31]. This was able to establish charge transfer from Au-Cu2+ and prevent charge transfer from the ligand to Au, which can also lead to the aggregation of gold nanoclusters [32], resulting in fluorescence quenching. The TEM image of Au NCs with Cu2+ also proved this (Fig. 2(b)), the originally well-dispersed Au NCs were tightly clustered together.

Second, as depicted in Fig. 5(a), the Au NCs fluorescence was restored with the increase of CP concentration. Moreover, the TEM image of Au NCs with Cu2+ and CP showed that Au NCs returned from the aggregated state to the monodisperse state again (Fig. 2(c)). We speculate that the Cu2+ was removed from the surface of Au NCs by competitive adsorption. Cu2+ has a higher affinity for -SH, which may allow CP interacted with the Cu2+ on the surface of Au NCs and forms Cu2+-S bonds.

Additionally, it can be explained by the UV–vis spectra. There was no significant change in the UV absorption spectrum before and after the addition of Cu2+ to Au NCs in Fig. S4. However, after the addition of CP, a slight red shift occurred in the UV–vis spectrum.

To estimate the capability of the established approach in real samples, some substances that may coexist in urine such as ions, sugar and amino acids were evaluated. In addition to CP, other substances including glucose (Glu), CH3COO–, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-, glycine (Gly), L-threonine (Thr), L-valine (Val), L-leucine (Leu), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), isoleucine (Ile), Na+, K+, Ca2+ and urea were selected as interferences. All interferents (1 mM) were present at 20-fold concentrations of the target analyte CP. Obviously, the other interfering substances in Fig. S5 had no effect on the fluorescent probe when coexisting with CP. This means that the sensing platform is highly resistant to interference for the detection of CP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CP in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: We detected CP in urine to assess the applicability of the method in real samples. The spiking and recovery tests were performed by adding different concentrations of CP to each sample. The results of the test strips for detecting CP in urine are shown in the Fig. 7 . Tables S1 and S2 represent the results of fluorescence spectroscopy, smartphone paper-based sensing platform for actual sample detection, respectively. The recovery of captopril in urine by fluorometric method was 96.0–103.3%. The recovery of CP in urine using a smartphone combined with a paper-based sensing platform was 92.0–108.0%. Moreover, the RSD (Relative Standard Deviation) of fluorescence spectroscopy and smartphone sensing platform were 3.2–4.2% and 5.1–7.8%, respectively. Although the accuracy of the smartphone sensing platform was not as good as that of the fluorescence spectroscopy, it can enable visualization and quantitative detection of CP on site. It can reliably detect the CP in real samples.

To conduct more research, we analyzed the amount of CP in urine samples of patients and healthy subjects after taking CP. The rejection of captopril occurred 1–5 h after dosing, with maximum rejection starting at 2.5 h [33]. After 2.5 h of taking the tablets, the urine of patients was collected for testing. Statistical comparisons of urine test results were performed using Student's t-test and F-test for ratios of variance at a 95% confidence level. As shown in Table S3, the contents of captopril in the urine of healthy subjects were 7.8 and 8.3 μM, and 4.5 and 5.2 μM in the urine of patients. The content of captopril in the urine samples of healthy subjects was higher than that of patients’ urine samples, which indicated that the absorption of CP was greater in patients. Furthermore, the results obtained by our method were compared with the standard method (potassium iodate titration) by Student’s t-test and showed no significant difference. It is proved that our proposed method has certain accuracy.

Compared with other methods for detecting CP, the results are shown in Table 1 . Shi et al. [34] prepared a hydrophilic hydroxypropyl-cyclodextrin cross-linked polymer (HP-CDP) and Xiao et al. [35] constructed a CP sensor with molybdenum oxide quantum dots (MoOxQDs), which based on fluorescence “off–on” strategy to achieve sensitive detection of CP. These methods had good selectivity and strong anti-interference ability. However, the fluorescence has only a single color change during the detection process, which has certain limitations. Silva et al. [36] used a graphite paste electrode (GPE) with rutin as the medium and Areias’s team [37] used copper(II) and bare glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) for the rapid and sensitive determination of captopril by cyclic voltammetry. Nevertheless, the method had a narrow linear range and high detection limit. The electrochemistry was susceptible to interference and had poor reproducibility. Long et al. [38] conducted quantitative detection of CP using a multiplicative effect model and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) with p-thiocresol as an internal standard. This method was low cost, simple operation and high sensitivity. Amini et al. [39] synthesized polyacrylonitrile/Ni-metal–organic backbone 74 (PAN/Ni-MOF-74) electrospun nanofibers, which was for spin-column micro-solid-phase extraction of CP. The linear range of high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was very wide and the detection limit was very low. The disadvantage was that the analysis was costly and must be performed in the laboratory. Wei’s team [40] used the formation of Ni(II) complexes with CP under alkaline conditions to detect CP by spectrophotometry. The advantages of this method were high accuracy, good reproducibility but serious interference. Nazifi et al. [41] used hollow MnO2 microspheres (HH-MnO2) as peroxidase to test CP by colorimetry. The method was fast and simple but the color change was single and subjective error existed in naked eye observation. Compared with these methods, the proposed method performed relatively well in terms of limit of detection. Although the detection limit was not as low as HPLC, it was portable and visual. This method had wider linear range than electrochemical methods. In addition, the proposed smartphone-assisted sensing platform can realize quantitative detection of CP. It provides a new method for rapid CP detection.

In summary, we established a novel fluorescent “on–off-on” strategy for the detection of CP, using ratiometric fluorescent test strips combined with a smartphone to achieve on-site visual analysis of CP. Dual-emitting fluorescent probe avoided environmental influences due to built-in calibration. The quenching mechanism of Cu2+ and Au NCs could be charge transfer and aggregation-induced quenching. CP can capture Cu2+ on the surface of Au NCs by competitive adsorption, leading to fluorescence recovery. In addition, the quenching and recovery mechanism of Au NCs fluorescence can be confirmed from TEM images and UV absorption spectra. More importantly, in actual urine sample analysis, both the fluorescence spectrometer and the smartphone platform showed satisfactory results. Therefore, the constructed smartphone-assisted sensing platform provides a rapid and easy approach for the visual quantitative assay of CP and has great potential in bioanalytical testing.

All applicable international, national, and/or institutional guidelines for the collection and use of urine were followed. All biological experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital.

Lixia Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ting Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Di Cui: Data curation, Investigation. Jiaqi Yi: Formal analysis, Investigation. Wei Jiang: Project administration, Investigation. Xiaoshuang Li: Resources, Investigation. Na Niu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ligang Chen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU03), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121562.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Galera, Development and validation of a capillary electrophoresis method with laser-induced fluorescence detection for the determination of captopril in human urine and pharmaceutical preparations, Electrophoresis, 27(2006) 2310-2316. http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/elps.200500861
